sentences.text: Until-- I was hiding in the apartment for a few days, but my suggestion is-- this, needless to say, is a central event of my experiences under the Germans which, in a way, has dictated a variety of things at a later date.
I have written a book, [NON-ENGLISH], which was published in Eretz, Israel, or Palestine, in 1947 or '46 by [NON-ENGLISH], describing in great detail this particular incident.
I also have written an article, which was published in the Ukraine in [NON-ENGLISH] Academy of Science.
And then, recently, I published a book, published in the United States by Fithian Press, and I described this particular instance in great detail.
Therefore, there is probably no need to dwell on it more.
No, I was hiding while these people were running around loose in the streets catching Jews.
And once this subsided-- I don't look particularly Jewish-- why, one day I decided, after two or three days, that's enough.
So, I washed up, put on a suit, and went out.
That's the end of it.
Well, she wasn't aware of that.
No.
It was a lengthy process because, you see, my mother knew that my father went to [NON-ENGLISH] to the Metropolitan.
He offered him shelter, and there was-- a rumor circulated that he was hidden by the Metropolitan, which I knew was not true because I've seen him shot.
But I didn't go out of my way to tell it to my mother.
The next day after this incident, two SS men came.
They had a list, and asked for my father.
So, my mother said he was taken away.
So, they beat her up and left.

And this was part and parcel of the [NON-ENGLISH] operation, namely, that a group of SS men was traveling to the occupied territories, and taking prominent people, according to the previously prepared lists, and executing them.
On this day, they executed [NON-ENGLISH], a prominent Polish writer, Bartel, former prime minister of Poland, prominent lawyers, Poles, leaders of the community, professors of university.
They just were aiming at decapitating a society.
My father was on this list and, therefore, they came to pick him up.
But he was already dead at that time.
Oh, a few months later.
She left the apartment, went to a district which was inhabited by non-Jews.
We had friends there.
The husband of this lady actually probably died in Brygidki, because he was a banker.
And we were staying there for two, three days, until the situation quieted itself in the city.
Yes.
No, she left and I joined her a few hours later.
That's right.
Was in a shock.
She was beaten up, after all.
My mother was, at that time, 33 years old, without any means, hunger, and three children.
I joined my mother in this district inhabited by non-Jews, on the other side of the city.
No.
No, because I don't look Jewish.
Well, I didn't look Jewish at that time.
No, that was not necessary.
I speak fluently Polish.
I didn't speak Ukrainian, and I speak Polish without a trace of an accent.
There were no rules.
There were no rules.
Well, I just kept my eyes open.
Whenever I saw commotion, I tried to move the other way.
Not yet.
No, everything was left alone.
The restrictions came very gradually.
The first thing they announced, the Jews are not allowed to use city parks.

Then they have closed a district, which was the most luxurious one, and have actually evacuated everybody, and this was designed for the German civilian population, which came to occupy administrative positions in the city, and for military.
And then they have enlarged this district and told the Jews to get out.
Oh, yes, because we intermingled.
So, the whole process, you see, of collecting Jews in the ghetto, was very gradual, and Jews never knew what the ghetto is.
Well, they went to their friends, and eventually the Judenrat was assigning apartments and dwellings to these people, and that's it.
Nothing.
Nothing, the apartment was intact.
They didn't rob anything, didn't take anything.
Oh, yes.
We went back there.
We lived there until Jews had to leave this particular district and move to a more restricted area, but that's much later.
You could be caught on the street and taken to forced labor, and either you came back or you didn't.
Oh, yes, many a time.
The railroad station.
We just dragged rails and all kinds of things.
Sometimes, if you're lucky, we worked for a German military units, and they made us work but they didn't beat us up.
If you were unlucky, you were beaten, and that's the end of it.
Just for a day.
Usually what happened is, they had trucks standing on the street, and they were catching men-- at that time only men-- taking them where it was need to do some work, whatever the work is.
Either to remove rubble from bombing, or to repair roads, or to work at the railroad station, which was very heavily bombed, and things of that sort.
I was caught three or four times.
And then I didn't like the idea, so I went out every morning at 4 o'clock, and I worked in outside of the city, far in the suburbs.
This was under the Soviets.
They had there sort of a cooperative producing vegetables, and I used to go there because there it was quiet and we were not beaten.
We volunteered there, and as I liked agriculture and things of that sort, I enjoyed working there.
No, no, no, they were locals.
But the locals had free labor.
We used to bring our own food, and that's it.
So it was a reasonably decent way to spend the day and go home at night.
Immediately.
Well, there were several Judenrats, and that is a complicated question.
And I think, again, this was very carefully described and studied, so I'd rather not go into details of the Judenrat mechanism.
Originally, these were leaders of the community, and they tried to protect the community.

About four weeks after the Germans arrived, the general in charge of the city-- infantry general, not an SS-- imposed an enormous ransom on the Jewish community, and silver and all the precious things, jewelry, had to be brought to the Judenrat.
Otherwise, reprisals were threatened.
So, this-- carpets, people brought it simply to buy peace, and this was transported, probably, to Germany.

This Judenrat was still organized and sensible group of people, headed by [NON-ENGLISH], Yousef [NON-ENGLISH] When they came to him and said, you have now to give us a few hundred people for labor camps, he refused.
He said, money, yes, cooperation, yes, human beings, no.
So, they took him to a prison and shot him with the rest of the Judenrat.
That's the end of the story, and then a new Judenrat showed up.
Who were the leaders-- who were picked to be Judenrat leaders?
There's no criteria--
I cannot answer that.
I really couldn't--
I was in the [NON-ENGLISH] Yousef [NON-ENGLISH] Judenrat.
My mother got me there, because while you were in the Judenrat you had a little piece of paper, so you were left alone.
And I was a clerk in the statistical department, where they were preparing lists.
This was, in my opinion, already set up to have better control of the Jewish population, and set up the mechanism for its destruction, eventual destruction.
Names and addresses.
No.
Address of a house on a street, and who the Jews are who dwell there.
End of story.
I was not there, because I was taken with other clerks to the same prison, and I escaped from there.
But that's, again, a long story.
It's described in my book.
The name of the person was [NON-ENGLISH].
And whoever entered [NON-ENGLISH] rarely came out alive.
Well, they came with trucks, loaded us on trucks, brought us to the prison.
At the Judenrat.
Yes, there was several buildings because the whole administration had to be organized.
It was what-- [NON-ENGLISH], labor.
[NON-ENGLISH] means a labor department.
No, but-- the name of the prison.
[NON-ENGLISH] It's a Polish name.
[NON-ENGLISH].
It's an obscure name.
About two, three days, and we were taken out to be shot.
No.
I jumped out of the truck.
There were some others.
I was not.
Some were hurt, were shot.
Yeah.
They took people to Piaski, which was a very notorious place of execution by the Germans at that time.
Piaski.
Suburb-- it was essentially a sand quarry.
That's right.
Home.
[LAUGHS]
That's right.
Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.
No, no, no.
Once you got out, you got out.
After that, I was still hanging around the city for a while and observing the situation.
I came to conclusion that I have to get out, and I want to Rawa Ruska.
No, no.
Rawa Ruska was a strange city.
It was a sea of the local administration of the districts, which means the court, schools, police, under the Poles.
And the Jewish community, primarily Jew-- maybe 80% of the population was Jewish.
It was a service town.
It's livelihood was strange, because they were importing scraps of furs from Leipzig, which was a very important fur center.
And it was fashionable in central Europe, and to some extent western Europe, too, for women to wear the lamb's coat, lamb's fur coat.
So, the scraps as you cut the skins were packed into huge sacks, were sent to this town, and there the Jews were sitting and sewing it together into units which were shipped back to Leipzig.
That is the way they were eking out a living.
By the way, how far is Rawa Ruska from Lwow?
About 50 miles.
I went there with a cousin of my mother, and we were there-- we got ourselves a job as collectors of rags, scrap metal, and it was a subsidiary of Goering's workers.
Hermann Goering created an industrial empire for himself in Germany, and Austria, and Czechoslovakia and, in addition to that, there were subsidiaries.
And one of these civilian Germans was running a subsidiary, which was collecting rags, glass, scrap iron.
In Rawa Ruska?
I lived in the office, which means there was a little office, and there I had my little corner, and at night I had two blankets, and that's it.
In Rawa Ruska.
No, just in the city, center of the city.
A tiny little office.
Obviously, yes.
Well, we worked there-- there there were no Germans in this whole-- it was run by Jews and some Poles, so it was no problem.
You were not paid anything, but we had what was called good papers, which allowed us not to be caught.
What you refer to as [NON-ENGLISH], Because it was vulgarly called as [NON-ENGLISH].
[GERMAN] in German means useful, [GERMAN], Jew-- a useful Jew.
And even there was an aluminum plaque to that effect.
Oh, yes, I have it here.
Yes, I don't have the placard.
Every Jew had to wear, under penalty of death, an arm band with the Star of David.
As much as I wanted to.

It was not really the work-- we paid the Germans and the Poles for the papers, and we probably just were delivering symbolically some scraps here to elsewhere, which were collected by Jews whose livelihood this was before the war.
That's very hard to answer.
You had to just simply, in a way, take care of yourself.
The point is, that while in Lwow there was continuous pressure, continuous violence, and presence of Germans, and constant danger.
In Rawa Ruska, they paid off the five or six German policemen, and the Ukrainian police was controlled by the Germans, who kept them in check.
So, there was no violence when I arrived there for a few months.
Then the whole thing started there, too, but at a much lower pace.
All the time, because the post was functioning normally, and I got myself permits, which was rather unusual, to travel by railroad.
So, I used to go to Lwow every two, three months to visit my mother.
Oh, yes, yes.
It was very dangerous, because I could be suddenly caught and people, say, take my documents away, tear them, and shoot me.
So, it was taking my life into my hands each and every time I went on the railroad.
Yes, but it was not closed yet.
Slowly, it was the same process.
Namely, removal of Jews from the luxurious areas.
Then, squeezing them into smaller, and smaller area.
But the ghetto was not yet defined or closed.
Yes.
Before my escape.
Yes.
No, my mother moved first to one place, and then to the final ghetto.
She moved twice.
Yes.
I was in Rawa Ruska.
Regularly, stayed there a few days, yes.
She lived-- the first move was to what used to be a Jewish quarter.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Actually, if you are interested, this was a square.
They used to sell fish in huge wooden tubs.
It was not too far from the orthodox synagogue.
She has done it on her own, and she moved only few possessions because the furniture, everything else, was left behind.
It was impossible to take with the limitation.
The second move was into the final ghetto.
I escaped shortly before it was closed.
For two weeks.
No, no, I wasn't there.
The second place was one apartment shared by three families.
So, first she lived in an apartment by herself with the children.
Then she shared an apartment with three more families.
And then finally, everybody had three square meters assigned.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
In their first apartment, it was the widow of my uncle, who was shot with my father, also a prominent rabbi, his daughter, who was a child, and some other people whom I don't any longer remember.
The final was the director of the gymnasium which I attended, and his wife, and many other people I don't really recall.
Very well.
Much better than people here in New York, despite all these extraordinary pressures.
Furthermore, they were clean.
They didn't run that around in jeans and look like refugees, like people in New York.
They, under the circumstances, still maintained human dignity.
Yes.
No, I didn't attend, but I'm aware.
But why did people get married?

A sister of my friend, a very beautiful girl, much older-- slightly older than I, two or three years, married a Jewish policeman because, by marrying him, she was taken out of the [NON-ENGLISH], probably for three or four weeks.
No, no, no, no.
Forget about it.
Not in Lwow, because--
They buried him.
The cemetery.
No, no, they had Jewish cemeteries.
Yes.
Not really.
You see, you ask questions about expressions of normal community life.

These are abnormal circumstances, and all I can tell you is, in order to put it in a proper perspective, try to imagine a pressure cooker with heat constantly increased, and people lived, literally, by the hour.
Yes.
That is a very complicated and a very delicate question.
Why?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the Jewish police and its command, there were some very decent people who tried their best to help, and alleviate the suffering.
But, don't forget that they were created as a mechanism to keep order in the community, keep it quiet and docile.
Eventually, their job was to identify where Jews live, and make it possible for the Germans to collect them in places where they were sent to the death camp.
No, I had no experience because, you see, I moved my own way.
There were also those who where [NON-ENGLISH] men of the Gestapo.
Men who worked for the Gestapo, who were agents of the Gestapo.
Oh, surely, and one of them, a prominent Jewish real estate operator here in New York, Bruce [NON-ENGLISH], was one of them.

What his role was I don't know, but I know that he was one of the movers and shakers in the Jewish community in the last Judenrats, which were essentially a mechanism to collect and destroy the community.
Not in the beginning.
Eventually, yes.
I was there when the wall was erected, but I wasn't there when they closed because I didn't like to wait for that.
Well, they used, usually, the area.
For example, there was a railroad track which separated the ghetto from the rest of the city, and the rest was fenced in.
Wooden planks--
Yes.
Wire.
Barbed wire.
Pardon me?
Always.
There were one or two entrances, simply because people used to march to work in the morning and come back in the evening.
Jewish police, sometimes Ukrainian police.
So, when you speak about the Jewish police, you see, it's very hard to say that they were all collaborators, and murderous, and henchmen.
Not necessarily.
It's very fairly complicated subject.
Surely.
